preamble.summary: A wisdom psalm that pronounces blessing on the person who fears the LORD and walks in his ways. The blessings are domestic and concrete: eating the fruit of your own labor, a wife like a fruitful vine in the inner rooms of your house, children like olive shoots around your table. The psalm extends the blessing outward from household to nation — may you see Jerusalem prosper, may you see your grandchildren, and may peace rest on Israel.
preamble.remarkable: After Psalm 127's declaration that human effort without God is futile, Psalm 128 describes what human effort with God looks like: it looks ordinary. The blessings here are not spectacular — no military victories, no prophetic visions, no miraculous interventions. They are a meal earned by your own hands, a spouse, children, and long life. The psalm sanctifies the mundane. The image of the wife as a gefen poriyyah ('fruitful vine') in the inner rooms (yarkete) of the house and children as shitilei zetim ('olive shoots') around the table creates a picture of organic, living abundance. Vines and olives are the signature crops of the land of Israel; both take years to mature and produce for generations. The psalm says that a household rooted in the fear of the LORD grows like the land's most enduring plants.
preamble.friction: The psalm's domestic vision reflects ancient Israelite social structures — the male householder as the addressed audience, the wife described in relation to the home's interior. Modern readers may find this limiting. The Hebrew text, however, is not prescriptive about gender roles so much as descriptive of the ancient household as an economic and spiritual unit. The 'fear of the LORD' (yirat YHWH) that opens the psalm is the foundation — everything that follows is presented as the fruit of that posture, not as a guaranteed formula.
preamble.connections: This psalm pairs with Psalm 127 as a wisdom diptych on household and family. Where 127 warns against self-reliance, 128 describes the blessings of God-centered living. The vine and olive imagery echoes Psalm 52:8 ('I am like a green olive tree in the house of God') and Hosea 14:6-7 (Israel's restored beauty compared to olive trees and vines). The closing shalom al Yisrael matches Psalm 125:5 exactly.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֗יר הַֽמַּ֫עֲל֥וֹת אַ֭שְׁרֵי כׇּל־יְרֵ֣א יְהוָ֑ה הַ֝הֹלֵ֗ךְ בִּדְרָכָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed is every one that feareth the LORD; that walketh in his ways.
verses.rendering:
A song of ascents.
Blessed is everyone who fears the LORD,
who walks in his ways.
translator_notes: The beatitude ashrei ('blessed, happy, fortunate') opens this psalm as it opens Psalm 1, creating a wisdom-literature frame. The phrase kol yere YHWH ('everyone who fears the LORD') is universal — the blessing is not limited to priests, kings, or prophets but available to anyone who lives in reverent awareness of God. The parallel haholekh bidrakav ('who walks in his ways') translates fear into action: to fear God is to walk God's path.

verses.text_hebrew: יְגִ֣יעַ כַּ֭פֶּיךָ כִּ֣י תֹאכֵ֑ל אַ֝שְׁרֶ֗יךָ וְט֣וֹב לָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou shalt eat the labour of thine hands: happy shalt thou be, and it shall be well with thee.
verses.rendering:
You will eat the fruit of your own labor —
blessed are you, and it will go well for you.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase yegia kappekha ('the labor of your hands') refers to work done with one's own hands — agricultural toil, craftsmanship, honest productive effort. The blessing is strikingly simple: ki tokhel ('when you eat') means you will actually get to consume what you produce. In a world of taxation, confiscation, invasion, and famine, this was not guaranteed. The ability to eat your own harvest was a sign of peace and divine favor (cf. Deuteronomy 28:33, where the curse is that a foreign nation eats your produce). The double benediction ashrekha ve-tov lakh ('blessed are you and good for you') piles up affirmation.
translator_notes: The blessing of eating one's own labor directly counters the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:30-33 and Leviticus 26:16, where enemy nations consume what Israel plants. To eat your own food in peace is itself a theological statement: the curse has been reversed.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶשְׁתְּךָ֤ ׀ כְּגֶ֥פֶן פֹּרִיָּה֮ בְּיַרְכְּתֵ֢י בֵ֫יתֶ֥ךָ בָּ֭נֶיךָ כִּשְׁתִלֵ֣י זֵיתִ֑ים סָ֝בִ֗יב לְשֻׁלְחָנֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy wife shall be as a fruitful vine by the sides of thine house: thy children like olive plants round about thy table.
verses.rendering:
Your wife will be like a fruitful vine
in the inner rooms of your house;
your children like olive shoots
around your table.
verses.expanded_rendering: The vine (gefen) is one of Israel's signature blessings — along with grain and oil, it represents the fullness of the land's produce. A fruitful vine (gefen poriyyah) is one that bears abundantly. The location be-yarkete beiteka ('in the inner rooms/recesses of your house') places the wife at the heart of the home — yarkete means the innermost or most protected area, the deep interior. This is not a statement about restriction but about centrality: the vine grows in the protected, nourishing center. The children as shitilei zetim ('olive shoots') uses the image of young olive saplings growing around the base of a mature tree. Olive trees live for centuries and their shoots represent continuity and new growth. The table (shulchan) is the gathering point — the family eats together, and the shoots encircle it.
translator_notes: The word yarkete ('sides, recesses, innermost parts') appears in other contexts to describe the innermost parts of a cave (1 Samuel 24:3) or the far reaches of a territory (Isaiah 14:13). In the domestic setting, it indicates the protected interior of the house — the private quarters rather than the public-facing areas.

key_terms.semantic_range: transplanted saplings, young planted olive trees, fresh shoots
key_terms.note: shitilei derives from shatal ('to plant, to transplant'). Olive trees were the most enduring and economically vital trees in ancient Israel — they could live over a thousand years, and their oil was used for food, light, medicine, and anointing. Young olive shoots growing around the parent tree's base symbolize generational continuity and God-given abundance.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֣ה כִי־כֵ֭ן יְבֹ֣רַךְ גָּ֑בֶר יְ֝רֵ֗א יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, that thus shall the man be blessed that feareth the LORD.
verses.rendering:
Look — this is how the man is blessed
who fears the LORD.
translator_notes: The particle hinneh ('look') draws attention to the preceding verses as evidence: this domestic abundance is what blessing looks like when it flows from the fear of the LORD. The psalm presents no extraordinary gifts — only a working household, food, a spouse, and children. The implication is that the ordinary, when rooted in God, is the blessing.

verses.text_hebrew: יְבָרֶכְךָ֥ יְהוָ֗ה מִצִּ֫יּ֥וֹן וּ֭רְאֵה בְּט֣וּב יְרוּשָׁלָ֑͏ִם כֹּ֖ל יְמֵ֣י חַיֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD shall bless thee out of Zion: and thou shalt see the good of Jerusalem all the days of thy life.
translator_notes: The blessing now radiates outward from the household to the city. The source of blessing is mi-Tsiyon ('from Zion') — the temple mount, God's dwelling place. The prayer u-re'eh be-tuv Yerushalayim ('and may you see the good/prosperity of Jerusalem') expresses the hope that the individual will live long enough and in enough peace to witness the city flourishing. Personal blessing and national blessing are linked: your household's abundance is part of Jerusalem's abundance.

verses.text_kjv: Yea, thou shalt see thy children's children, and peace upon Israel.
verses.rendering:
May you see your children's children.
Peace on Israel.
translator_notes: The final blessing is generational: re'eh vanim le-vanekha ('see children belonging to your children') — grandchildren are the visible proof that God's blessing extends beyond one lifetime. The closing formula shalom al Yisrael ('peace on Israel') matches Psalm 125:5, framing these central Songs of Ascents with an identical prayer. The movement from individual fear of God (v. 1) to national peace (v. 6) traces the psalm's conviction that personal faithfulness contributes to collective flourishing.
